title: Stop GREED Act of 2025
plain_summary: The Stop GREED Act of 2025 stops the Department of Veterans Affairs from giving special bonus money to high-level executives who work at the main VA offices in Washington DC. These bonuses are usually given to workers who have skills that are hard to find or in high demand. Executives at other VA locations can still get bonuses, but only if they get approval from six important officials and only one person at a time. The head of the VA must tell Congress every year which executives got these bonuses.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Veterans' Affairs.
subjects: Government employee pay, benefits, personnel management

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Stop Government Rewards Enriching Executives in the District Act of two thousand twenty five or the Stop GREED Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Limitations on Provision of Incentives for Critical Skills to Senior Executive Service Employees of Department of Veterans Affairs
sections.text: This section changes the law about special pay bonuses called critical skill incentives that can be given to high level employees at the Department of Veterans Affairs. Senior executives who work at the main headquarters office in Washington cannot receive these special bonuses at all. This includes executives at the Veterans Health Administration, Veterans Benefits Administration, and National Cemetery Administration headquarters, no matter where they actually do their work. Senior executives who work outside the headquarters can only get these bonuses under strict rules. Each bonus must be approved individually, not given to groups of employees. Six different high level officials must all approve each bonus, including the Under Secretaries, the Assistant Secretary for Human Resources, the Director of Management or Chief Financial Officer, the Assistant Secretary for Accountability and Whistleblower Protection, and the General Counsel. If a senior executive works mostly at headquarters but also works at other Veterans Affairs facilities, they can only get a bonus for the portion of their job done outside headquarters. The bonus amount must match the percentage of time they spend at non headquarters locations. Starting one year after this law passes and every year after that, the Secretary of Veterans Affairs must send a report to Congress about which senior executives received these bonuses. The report can be included in other required reports to Congress. A Senior Executive Service position means the same thing as defined in title five section three thousand one hundred thirty two of federal law.
